entry.title: Alphanumeric CNPJ: What You Need to Know
entry.content:
Brazil’s tax reform introduces several structural changes across the fiscal landscape, and one of the most impactful is the adoption of the alphanumeric CNPJ. As this new identifier takes shape, SAP systems must be ready.

This blog provides an overview of the change, explains why it is being introduced, outlines the SAP areas affected, and highlights the delivery timelines and key references you should be aware of to prepare for compliance.

What Is Changing?

The Brazilian CNPJ (National Register of Legal Entities) has always been a 14-digit numeric identifier. Starting in July 2026, it will expand to include both letters and numbers, becoming an alphanumeric identifier. This change applies only to newly issued CNPJs, existing ones remain unchanged.

This change aims to:

  • Increase the available combination space for taxpayer identification
  • Improve the classification of entities, branches, and establishments
  • Provide more flexibility and scalability for the new tax model

SAP's Preparation and Timeline

SAP is actively updating its solutions to accommodate the new format. The central reference is SAP Note 3714871 – Tax Reform: Alphanumeric CNPJ Leading SAP Note, which consolidates guidance, impacted objects, delivery plans, and related documentation.

This is the primary source you should follow.

Delivery plans can be accessed through the following links:

  • Tax Reform - Alphanumeric CNPJ - Normative Instruction No. 2229, of October 15,
  • Tax Reform - Joint Technical Note 2025.001 - Alphanumeric CNPJ

Key Areas Affected

SAP has identified several modules and processes affected by the new CNPJ format. Key areas include:

  • Business Partner: Fields now support alphanumeric input with updated validation rules and error messages
    • Adjustment delivered in SAP Note 3583627
  • Logistics and Nota Fiscal Database: Structural updates to tables, NF-e repository, integration frameworks and tax transactions
  • Document and Reporting Compliance (NF-e): Updates to electronic nota fiscal, digital signature, and schema validations
  • Human Capital Management (HCM) Brazil (Payroll): Adaptation of eSocial solution (layouts, event tables, and extraction process), employer records, employee master data and HCM reports
  • Finance and Banking: Changes to CNAB files, PIX, TED, and Boleto payment methods, updates in routines for generating payment files

SAP System Changes in Customizing (IMG)

Adjustments already delivered within the SAP Customizing environment include:

Global Company Parameters

SPRO → Financial Accounting → Financial Accounting Global Settings → Global Parameters for Company Code → Maintain Additional Parameters

Business Place Definition

SPRO → Cross-Application Components → General Application Functions → Nota fiscal  →  CNPJ Business Places  →  Define Business Places for Brazil

These areas now fully support alphanumeric CNPJ values.

ABAP Objects Impacted

SAP Note 3714871 provides a detailed list of ABAP objects expected to be modified. These objects are being converted from numeric to character-based (CHAR) fields to support the new identifier.

SAP recommends that you review custom developments (Z objects) and integrations that reference numeric CNPJ fields, and other objects that contain the CNPJ (access key), as these may need adjustments.

This includes:

  • Tables
  • Data elements
  • Domain definitions
  • Interfaces
  • Business Add-In (BAdI) implementations
  • Enhancements
  • Custom reports

Planned Delivery Timeline

Important: The expected delivery dates are subject to change without prior notice and represent only an estimate provided by SAP, not a contractual commitment for delivery.

HCM Brazil (Payroll)

  • Phase 1: March 13, 2026
  • Phase 2: March 31, 2026

Logistics and Nota Fiscal Database Adjustments

  • April 17, 2026 (Prerequisite for all other Logistics, Document and Reporting Compliance, and Financial updates)

Document and Reporting Compliance (NF-e) – Public Cloud and Private Cloud/On-Premise

  • April 17, 2026

Business Partner and Statutory Reports

  • April 17, 2026

Banking Files – CNAB / Payment Methods

  • CNAB Private Cloud/On-Premise: April 16, 2026FICA Private Cloud/On-Premise (PIX, TED, Boleto included): April 16, 2026

Key References

SAP Note

  • 3714871 – Tax Reform: Alphanumeric CNPJ Leading SAP Note

Regulatory Change Manager Links

  • HCM Impacts – Phase 1 and Phase 2
  • Nota Fiscal Database Adjustments
  • Business Partner and Statutory Reports
  • CNAB Files
  • CNAB Files FICA

Note: The links provided are specific to the Product Impact of SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition. For other products, such as SAP S/4HANA , SAP ERP Central Component, and SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, refer to the regulatory change manager links related to each product.

What Should You Do Now?

Start by reviewing SAP Note 3714871 and evaluating any custom developments that reference CNPJ fields, and other objects that contain the CNPJ (access key). Early preparation will help minimize disruption and ensure your organization stays compliant as Brazil's tax infrastructure evolves.

In summary, your action plan should be:

  1. Review SAP Note 3714871 in detail
  2. Evaluate all custom developments that reference CNPJ fields, and other objects that contain the CNPJ (access key)
  3. Track delivery updates via the regulatory change manager
  4. Plan for early testing in sandbox environment
  5. Update internal processes and integrations (banks, partners, NF-e service providers, tax engines, etc.)

Early preparation will help you avoid disruption and maintain compliance as Brazil's tax landscape evolves. The clock is ticking, but with the right approach, you can navigate this transition smoothly.

Update 27 April, 2026

Communication Regarding Planned Delivery

As part of a recent technical validation, an incompatibility was identified that requires a revision of the originally planned approach. To ensure system consistency and protect your landscape, the corresponding change has been temporarily reverted. At this time, no action is required from you. 

A new solution, based on an improved technical approach, is currently under development and will be made available shortly. We appreciate your understanding and reaffirm our commitment to delivering stable, high-quality solutions.

Update 06 May, 2026

SAP Notes have been released delivering a new solution based on an improved technical approach. You should review and follow the instructions provided in SAP Note 3714871 – Tax Reform: Alphanumeric CNPJ Leading SAP Note.

For more information or if you have any questions about the Brazilian tax reform,  visit the dedicated Tax Reform Work Zone — your hub for updates, resources, and deeper insights.

Did you enjoy this post? Give it a Like and share it with your colleagues! Feel free to leave your feedback or comments in the space below. And remember to follow the SAP S/4HANA Logistics for Brazil tag in the SAP Community to stay tuned for the latest news. 

 
Thanks for reading — until next time!
Luize

entry.title: I reverse‑engineered MM invoice accounting (and here's your new automatic analysis tool!)
entry.content:
I’ve spent the last few years in SAP Support staring at MM invoices that “don’t make sense.”
If you recognize subjects like:

  • “GRIR can not be cleared”

  • “Posting and reversal amount does not match”
  • “Exchange rate difference between GR and IR settling to material account”
  • “Unexpected UMB revaluation posting in MIRO”

…then you already know the kind of stories I’m talking about.

In theory, GR/IR should be simple: goods go in, invoice comes later, the clearing account should end up at zero and reversals should “just mirror” the original. In practice, you add price changes, condition updates, partial goods receipts, provisional/final invoicing, several currencies, and postings across periods – and suddenly the GR/IR line or the reversal document looks like it came from another universe.

For a long time, all we could do would be to share KBAs explaining the standard design and the complex calculations that actually occur in the back-end. This is not efficient, and increases both engineer effort and customer effort.

So I decided to do something slightly extreme: reverse‑engineer the MM invoice accounting logic and encode it into a report that tells the story for you.

The old way

If you’ve ever tried to really understand a tricky MIRO or MR8M document, you’ve probably met some of these notes:

  • 1674290 – Invoice Posting Logic – SAP ERP & SAP S/4HANA

  • 1609927 – Credit Memo Posting Logic – SAP ERP & SAP S/4HANA
  • 1789924 – Subsequent Debit/Credit Posting Logic – SAP ERP & SAP S/4HANA
  • 3382819 – MR11/MR11SHOW: Calculation logic for GR/IR clearing – SAP ERP & SAP S/4HANA
  • 3039200 – MIRO: KDM and PRD differences – SAP ERP & SAP S/4HANA

Together, they explain the core clearing logic very well: which quantities and values are used, how WRX is derived, what happens when subsequent debits/credits are handled, what's different in MR11, and how KDM/PRD differences are supposed to look.

The problem is not the documentation; the problem is how much work it takes to apply it:

  • You need to dig out all GR and IR documents for the PO item.

  • You need to consider the right currency context.
  • You need to apply the formulas on top of the customer’s specific history.
  • And you need to do it again for the next incident.

This is exactly where the older IV_WRX_ANALYSIS report was already a big step forward: it automated the WRX clearing calculation and showed that the system was simply following its own rules.

But that approach had some important limits:

  • It focused on WRX only (GR/IR account line).

  • It worked mainly in 1st local currency.
  • It concentrated on the final calculation, not on the whole story behind the document.

At some point I realized: for the kind of questions we see today, “WRX only” is not enough.

What consultants actually ask us

Here is how MM invoice issues usually arrive in Support:

  • “GRIR quantity is equal but PO is open due to currency conversion.”

  • “GRs and IRs imbalance at LC3 level while LC1/LC2 are fine.”
  • “Exchange rate difference between GR and IR is settling to material account; we expected KDM.”
  • “MIRO posting triggers UMB account and we want to know why.”
  • “MR8M: Posting and reversal amount does not match”
  • “New 0‑value line in local currency (LC3) in all VIM invoices after an upgrade.”

Notice what’s going on here:

  • Customers rarely say “Please show me the formula from Note 1674290.”

  • They say “Why is this GR/IR line so strange?” or “Why is this reversal not a mirror?”

All these scenarios have one thing in common:
You can answer them with the existing notes and manual calculations – but only if you’re ready to become a part‑time human calculator.

I wanted something better for consultants: a way to get the same level of explanation without having to rebuild all equations every time.

What I decided to build instead

Therefore, it is not enough to show to the consultants how WRX was calculated. It would be much better to access the whole story behind the invoice:

  • Which documents were taken into account.

  • How quantities and values accumulated over time.
  • How the system split the outcome into stock (BSX), price differences (PRD), exchange‑rate differences (KDM), GR/IR clearing (WRX), and sometimes revaluation (UMB) and other lines.
  • And, specially, why the accounting document is following standard design and, even if the amounts look strange, the system knows it did the right thing

So I built a new analysis report with a very simple idea: If you’re going to trust the accounting document, let’s show you exactly what the system saw and how it used it.

Design goals were straightforward:

  1. Show the entire PO/SA history for the invoice item
    Not just a final WRX total, but a line‑by‑line list of relevant GRs and IRs, so it’s obvious which documents the system actually considered.

  2. Support multiple currencies
    Consultants can switch the report to document currency or up to three local currencies, which is crucial when GR/IR appears balanced in one currency but strange in another.
  3. Explain the logic, not just print numbers
    After the history tables, the report has a “brain” section that walks through the main steps of the posting logic – in a way that matches the KBAs, but applied to the specific case you’re analyzing.
  4. Keep the input simple
    On the selection screen you basically tell the report:
    • which invoice item to analyze,
    • which currency type you want to see, and
    • in case of multiple account assignment, which account assignment line.

  5. Make it easy to share and debug
    When a customer sends the report output back to SAP, the structure is friendly both for humans and for any future tooling that needs to understand what happened.

And because the older IV_WRX_ANALYSIS report was already useful, I didn’t throw it away: I used it as a basis for this new solution.

What the new report actually shows

Without going into technical field names, here’s how the output is organized and why it helps.

Part 1: The ingredients

  1. Context block – what are we analyzing?
    At the top you see the invoice number, fiscal year, item, the PO reference and the general context.

  2. Goods Receipt history – “how did we get here on the GR side?”
    The first table lists all relevant GR documents for that PO item, and explains how GR quantities and amounts contribute to the final totals.

  3. Invoice history – “what happened before this invoice?”
    The next table does the same for previous IRs (and subsequent debits/credits), including reversals.
    It shows how much has already been invoiced before the document you’re analyzing.

Part 2: The Recipe

  1. Logic explanation 
    This is the part that replaces pages of manual calculation:

    • It shows how the GR/IR clearing line is derived, so you can see why WRX doesn’t always match the item amount.

    • It explains how the system splits the amount into:

      • Stock postings (BSX),
      • Price differences (PRD),
      • Exchange‑rate differences (KDM),
      • and, when relevant, revaluation (UMB) and other lines such as EIN, EKG, FRE etc.

    You don’t have to know every detail of the formulas from the KBAs – the report is essentially doing those calculations for you and showing the intermediate steps.

Part 3: The results

Final amounts – “what should the FI document look like?”
At the end, the report prints the final calculated amounts per transaction key, in a format that mirrors the accounting document.
This is the part you can compare directly with the FI document. If they match, you’ve just proved that the system is behaving as designed (and you didn’t need a spreadsheet to get there).

Your turn

The report is intentionally not a monster transaction. It’s something you can run quickly whenever a document is questioned and you need to audit results and investigate documents that look wrong.

To keep things simple (and consistent with SAP Support processes), the report ZIV_ACCOUNTING_ANALYSIS is delivered via SAP Note 2427297 – Analysis report to explain IV accounting documents

From there you can:

  • Download the report source from the attachments of the note and implement it manually via SE38 in your system, as described in the note.

    • The report is not available for SNOTE implementation. For that reason, you need to get the attachment for manual implementation
  • Then run it like any other custom analysis tool, using the straightforward selection screen.

The note itself remains the official reference for obtaining and implementing the report; the blog post you’re reading is about why it exists and how it can help you as a consultant.

entry.title: What is a SAP currency conversion?
entry.content:
If you read my previous article on SAP plant reallocation, you will have seen how political, economic and global change and uncertainty drive the requirement for complex technical change in SAP customers' ERP systems. 

Over the past few years, organisations have had to navigate inflation, volatile exchange rates, geopolitical instability and significant economic uncertainty. In some markets, businesses have faced sustained pressure from weakening local currencies. In others, governments have introduced entirely new currencies or announced plans to adopt new ones. Bulgaria’s recent adoption of the Euro is a good example of how these changes can quickly move from political discussion to operational reality.

When these events happen, businesses need to respond quickly. This often creates a very specific SAP challenge. How do you change the currency structure of an SAP system that may have been operating in the same currency for years?

At first glance, this may not sound particularly challenging. SAP already supports multiple currencies and most organisations regularly process transactions in foreign currencies. However, changing the company code currency is a very different challenge altogether. Once a company code has been operating for years, its currency becomes deeply embedded within financial processes, transactional records and reporting structures.

This is where a SAP currency conversion project comes in.

What is SAP currency conversion?

At a high level, SAP currency conversion refers to changing the local currency of a company code and converting the associated financial and transactional data so that the business can continue operating under a new currency structure.

This may happen because a country adopts a new national currency, because a business wants to move away from an unstable local currency or because organisational restructuring requires financial realignment across different currencies.

Regardless of the reason, the challenge tends to be the same. The business needs to move to a new currency structure. Your SAP ERP system, however, still contains years of financial history built around the old one. This means organisations cannot simply change a configuration setting and move on.

A company code currency is tied to general ledger balances, open receivables, open payables, asset accounting records, controlling objects and historical financial transactions. Over time, this creates a significant amount of dependency across the system. When the Eurozone countries moved to the Euro in 1999, the average SAP system would have been relatively small. Over the past quarter of a century, many have grown significantly. It's not unusual for SAP production systems to be 5TB and have billions of lines of transactional data. Some large ERP systems are ten times that size.

Why is this difficult in SAP?

If an organisation simply changes the currency setting at company code level, historical transactions remain stored in the original currency while new transactions begin posting in the new one. That quickly creates inconsistencies across financial reporting, reconciliation processes and audit requirements.

Businesses still need access to historical financial records and finance teams need confidence that reporting remains accurate before and after the conversion. Asset values need to remain consistent, open items need to be handled correctly and controlling structures need to continue functioning as expected. Integrations with downstream systems may also need to be reviewed.

Like many SAP transformation challenges, the configuration change itself is often relatively small. The real complexity sits within the underlying data.

The traditional approach

Historically, organisations often approached currency conversion through manual restructuring.

This might involve creating new company codes, migrating selected balances, manually adjusting financial records and maintaining historical environments for reporting purposes. Perhaps using application-level migration methods like BAPIs via a tool like LSMW,

While this approach can work, it often creates operational overheads. Finance teams may spend large amounts of time reconciling balances and manually validating financial outputs. And from the perspective of the business user, it creates a break between the transactional activity in the new company and the historical activity in the previous company code, requiring parallel reporting structures for at least a year or two.

The traditional approach often solves the immediate issue, but it is difficult and creates perhaps unnecessary complexity in the process. In some cases, organisations even delay the conversion because the perceived risk feels too high.

The table-based transformation approach

A more modern approach involves transforming the underlying SAP data model at table level rather than manually rebuilding financial structures from scratch.

Instead of creating entirely new company codes and manually recreating financial records, organisations can use specialist landscape transformation technology such as Natuvion DCS to convert the relevant SAP data directly. This allows the existing company code structure to remain in place while the underlying financial data is converted to reflect the new currency model.

This typically includes converting company code currency assignments, general ledger balances, open items, asset accounting data, controlling objects and related financial records. Because these data objects are heavily interconnected, this requires a highly structured approach that ensures consistency across all affected tables.

Landscape transformation software enables organisations to analyse impacted tables, identify dependencies and execute the conversion in a controlled way while preserving historical financial integrity. Rather than manually rebuilding financial structures, organisations can convert existing data with significantly less disruption to day-to-day operations.

This can reduce downtime, minimise reconciliation effort and allow organisations to respond much faster when economic conditions create urgent change.

Final thoughts

Currency conversion may not be discussed as frequently as other SAP project types eg S/4HANA migration, but it is becoming increasingly relevant as businesses navigate economic volatility, regulatory change and global uncertainty.

The challenge is that what initially sounds like a simple finance decision can quickly become a major SAP transformation project if approached incorrectly. Traditional methods can still work, but they are often slower, more manual and far more disruptive than organisations expect.

Table-based transformation approaches are giving businesses a far more efficient way to adapt when currency structures need to change, while preserving the financial integrity that organisations depend on.

If you would like to read more on this topic, you can do so here.

entry.title: AI in SAP: Where Value Is Actually Being Created
entry.content:
Everybody has been talking about the massive transformations that we are witnessing in regards AI and enterprise systems. I'm not saying this is not true, I've seen it myself, especially in industries like energy, where operations are becoming more data-driven every day. In many ways, the impact is already there. My question is: where exactly is AI making a difference?

Because even though technology is moving fast, the impact doesn’t always show up at the same pace. In most cases, the problem isn’t whether AI works or not, because it usually does. The real challenge is what happens after the model produces an output, and whether that output actually turns into something the business can act on.

From what we’ve seen in different projects, many organizations are already experimenting with AI across their SAP landscape. Individual use cases are delivering valuable results that go from producing accurate outputs to outperforming traditional approaches. What happens when companies try to take that value beyond a single use case and extend it across processes? Do things start to get complicated?

The challenge usually doesn’t show up when the model is running. It appears a bit later, when teams try to use those outputs in every day operations. Insights might be accurate, but that doesn’t automatically mean they fit into how the business actually works. Maybe, instead of asking ourselves if something works, we should shift the conversation to how to actually use it in a consistent way. 

Fitting a model or an insight into a complex process that already exists is the real challenge. Most companies do not really have issues in generating insights: They can forecast better, process information faster, and detect patterns that were hard to see before. But what comes after? What happens once the insight is there?

In an SAP environment, acting on that insight is rarely a simple step. Even something as straightforward as improving a forecast can have countless effects across different areas. Adjusting inventory levels or procurement volumes doesn’t stay within a single function. It impacts planning, finance, logistics, and sometimes even customer commitments. Making that change actually happen requires aligned data, coordinated processes, and systems that can absorb the adjustment without breaking.

We’ve seen this take place very clearly in our work with a global energy company. The challenge there wasn’t a lack of data or visibility, but how to act on it in a way that was both fast and reliable. Inspecting thousands of kilometers of transmission lines manually was slow, risky, and difficult to scale. By introducing drones combined with AI-based image recognition, they were able to detect potential issues much earlier and speed up decision-making around maintenance.

 

Although the model identifying anomalies made a really big difference, it was how that information connected back into their SAP processes that changed everything. The insights generated in the field needed to trigger actions like maintenance planning and work order creation, without adding more manual steps. Once that connection was in place, the impact became much more tangible: inspections became significantly safer, productivity increased, and response times improved.

This is where a pattern starts to emerge. AI is already delivering value, but mostly in scenarios where the scope is controlled and the dependencies are limited. The challenge appears when organizations try to move beyond those contained use cases and bring AI into core operations.

At that point, the underlying SAP architecture starts to matter a lot more. Data consistency, integration, and process standardization are no longer background topics. They become the difference between an insight that looks good on paper and one that actually drives action. The more companies rely on AI-generated outputs, the more they depend on their systems being able to respond without friction.

What we’ve learned from these experiences is that the shift doesn’t happen when you add more models. It happens when those models become part of how processes run end to end. That’s when AI stops being something experimental and starts becoming part of everyday operations.

So, to answer my initial question: value is not created when a model detects an anomaly or improves a forecast. It’s created when that output leads to a decision that is actually executed. And in SAP environments, that step from insight to action is still where most of the real work is happening today.

AI is already showing what’s possible. But the organizations that will see the biggest impact are the ones that can connect that potential to how their systems actually run.

entry.title: How AI Helped Me Understand and Decompress SAP Content Server 7.5 CSCompress PDF Files
entry.content:
Building a Standalone CSCompress Decompressor for SAP Content Server with AI

When I first encountered a CSCompress file from SAP Content Server, I assumed there had to be a simple way to open it. There was not. This article tells the story of how AI helped me analyze the format, validate it against real compressed and original files, and create a standalone solution for decompressing SAP Content Server PDF files.

 

 

Figure 1. Shows HTML page that is used as standalone tool to decompress PDF files compressed with CSCompress (SAP Contet Server 7.5)

 

I already knew from the start what the real problem was: I had a PDF file stored in compressed form, and I wanted to get the original readable PDF back outside SAP.

Like many of us would do, I first tried the quick workaround. I renamed the file to .zip and attempted to open it as a normal archive. That did not work. The file remained just as unreadable as before, and it was clear that this was not a standard ZIP-based compression scenario.

So I turned to AI and asked it to help me analyze the compression format and find a way to decompress the file properly.

At that point, the challenge was perfectly clear:

take a CSCompress file from SAP Content Server, decompress it, and recover the original PDF.

And yes — I accepted the challenge. 😛

 

First Attempt: Analyze the Compressed File Directly

The first step was to inspect the compressed file itself and see whether the format could be recognized immediately.

It was clear quite quickly that the file was not a normal PDF anymore:

it did not start with a %PDF- header,
it could not be opened by standard archive utilities,
and the payload clearly had some SAP-specific wrapping.

AI helped me inspect the binary structure and reason about what the first bytes might represent, but one compressed file alone was not enough to fully understand the format with confidence.

At that point, the investigation needed a better strategy.

Switching to a Controlled Test Approach

Instead of trying to reverse-engineer everything from a single mysterious blob, I changed the method.

I asked AI to generate several known PDF files for testing. I then uploaded those files into SAP Content Server, allowed the system to compress them, and collected both sides of the puzzle:

the original PDF files
the matching CSCompress-compressed files

This gave me several original/compressed file pairs, which made the analysis much more reliable.

That was the turning point.

Once I had real pairs, I was no longer guessing in the dark. AI could now compare the files directly, validate assumptions, and check whether a decompression attempt really reconstructed the original file byte-for-byte.

AI was Looking for Technical Clues in Open Source SAP-Related Code

After comparing the sample pairs, AI started looking for technical clues in open-source SAP-related compression code.

This search eventually led to useful source files such as:

vpa105CsObjInt.cpp
hpa107cslzh.h

These files did not provide a ready-made standalone tool that AI could simply copy and run. But they did provide something just as valuable: structure and direction.

From that code, AI could confirm the structure of the CSCompress wrapper header and extract important details such as:

where the original length is stored,
how the algorithm is encoded,
and what fixed marker bytes appear in the compressed file.

At that point, the work for AI became a combination of:

open-source code reading,
binary comparison of real files,
and AI-assisted reasoning and validation.
Understanding the Header

The validated sample files used in my tests all followed the same general structure.

The compressed document begins with an 8-byte CSCompress header:

bytes 0..3: original uncompressed file length, stored as a little-endian 32-bit integer
byte 4: combined version/algorithm field
bytes 5..6: magic bytes 1F 9D
byte 7: a special field used in this compression variant

For example, a header like this:

F4 06 00 00 12 1F 9D 02

can be interpreted as:

original file size = 0x000006F4
version = 1
algorithm = 2
magic = 1F 9D
special = 2

That was a major milestone, because it meant the file was no longer an opaque mystery. It had a recognizable wrapper and clearly structured metadata.

What the Sample Pairs Proved

The original/compressed file pairs turned out to be essential.

They allowed AI to confirm that:

the first four bytes really represented the original file length,
the test files consistently used algorithm 2,
the same structure repeated across multiple examples,
and, most importantly, the decompressed output could be verified against the original file content exactly.

This is worth emphasizing:

without the open-source SAP-related code, AI would not have understood the header and format direction so quickly;

without the real original/compressed sample pairs, AI would not have been able to prove that the standalone decompression logic actually worked on the real SAP Content Server output.

Both parts were necessary.

From Analysis to Implementation

Once the format was understood well enough, the next step was implementation.

The first working version was written in Python, because Python is ideal for binary parsing, rapid experimentation, and repeated validation.

That version was very useful for proving that the decompression logic worked. It allowed AI to:

parse the header,
isolate the payload,
apply the required bit-level handling,
decompress the content,
and compare the output with the original PDF.

After that, I explored additional standalone implementations in other languages and formats.

The decompressor was then implemented in:

Python
PHP
HTML/JavaScript

I also experimented with VBScript-based Windows usability, mainly to make the tool easier to use in desktop environments. In practice, the most convenient result turned out to be the HTML/JavaScript standalone version, because it:

runs by simple double-click,
requires no SAP system,
requires no external libraries,
and works comfortably on Windows through the browser.

That version later evolved further and was enhanced to calculate:

SHA-256
SHA-1
MD5

for both the input and output files, making validation easier and more transparent.

What AI Actually Contributed

I want to be precise here, because that matters.

AI simply hand me a perfect ready-made decompression function on the first try.

What AI did help with was:

analyzing the structure of the compressed files,
identifying patterns in the headers,
reasoning about what certain bytes might represent,
looking in open-source SAP-related code,
and turning those clues into practical standalone tools.

In other words, AI was used as a Senior Developer.
AI model used here was: GPT-5.4 Thinking

The final solution came from combining:

AI-assisted analysis,
AI open-source code reading,
real SAP Content Server sample pairs,
and repeated testing until the output matched the original files exactly.


Current Scope of the Solution

The current implementation is validated using PDF file pairs, because that was the real-life use case I was working on.

Technically, the decompression logic operates on the CSCompress container format, not on PDF syntax alone. That means the same logic may also work for other file types compressed with the same CSCompress variant.

However, the current repository and validation examples are centered on PDF-based test cases, and I think it is important to state that clearly.

Everything Is Published on GitHub

To make the work reproducible and useful for others, I published the project on GitHub.

The repository includes:

the standalone decompressor implementations
the validation examples
and the supporting material used during the investigation

GitHub repository:

CSCompress_Decompressor

Conclusion

What started as a practical need — “I know this is a compressed PDF, I just want the original file back” — turned into a very enjoyable technical investigation.

The interesting part was not only finding a way to decompress the file, but understanding enough of the format to build standalone tools that work outside SAP.

In the end, the solution was not the result of one magic trick. It was the result of:

trying the obvious shortcut,
failing,
building controlled test samples,
AI reading the right technical clues, 
AI validating against real file pairs,
and iterating until the output was correct.

That is also what made the process fun.

If you have worked with SAP Content Server internals, ArchiveLink, CSCompress files, or similar reverse-engineering tasks, feel free to share your thoughts, comments, or improvements. I would be very interested to hear how others approached similar problems.

entry.title: Deep Dive into ARFCSDATA Table Growth & Cleanup Strategy
entry.content:
The Problem: A Silent Database Killer

During a routine database health check, we discovered something alarming — the ARFCSDATA table had grown to a size that was consuming a significant portion of our SAP system's database. The total entry count had ballooned to over 847 million rows, with a staggering 827 million of those entries belonging to a single RFC destination: <BWSID>CLNT100.

This kind of uncontrolled table growth is one of those issues that develops silently over months, often going unnoticed until it starts impacting system performance or storage costs.

 

Understanding ARFCSDATA and ARFCSSTATE

Before diving into the root cause, it helps to understand what these tables do:

  • ARFCSSTATE — Stores the state/header records for transactional RFC (tRFC) and queued RFC (qRFC) calls. Each entry represents a Logical Unit of Work (LUW) identified by a Transaction ID (TID).
  • ARFCSDATA — Stores the actual payload data for each tRFC/qRFC LUW. A single TID in ARFCSSTATE can correspond to multiple entries in ARFCSDATA depending on the number of function modules called within that LUW.

Under normal conditions, these entries are created, processed, and then cleaned up automatically. When cleanup fails — typically because the target system never acknowledges receipt — the data accumulates indefinitely.

Root Cause Analysis: The SQL Investigation

To find out what was causing the bloat, we ran a diagnostic query joining ARFCSSTATE and ARFCSDATA:

 
sql - Reference note 2899366.
SELECT a.arfcipid, a.arfcpid, a.arfctime, a.arfctidcnt, COUNT(*)
FROM arfcsstate AS aLEFT JOIN arfcsdata AS b  ON a.arfcipid = b.arfcipid  AND a.arfcpid = b.arfcpid  AND a.arfctime = b.arfctime  AND a.arfctidcnt = b.arfctidcntGROUP BY a.arfcipid, a.arfcpid, a.arfctime, a.arfctidcntORDER BY COUNT(a.arfcipid) DESC

Query Result:

The results were eye-opening. The top single TID entry had over 3.6 million corresponding rows in ARFCSDATA. The top 30 entries alone accounted for hundreds of millions of rows, with most individual TIDs carrying between 3.1 million and 3.7 million data entries each.

We extracted the top 350 TIDs from ARFCSSTATE for further analysis. The findings were consistent: all these entries pointed to the BW (Business Warehouse) system destination, and the username associated with them was used for data extraction processes.

This pointed directly to a BW extraction scenario where tRFC calls from the source system (ECC) to the BW system were not being acknowledged and cleaned up, causing both the state and data tables to fill up with stale records.

SM58: The Smoking Gun

A check of SM58 (tRFC Monitor) on the ECC system confirmed the diagnosis. We found approximately 2,200 stuck tRFC entries, accumulated over an extended period:

  • Some were in Executing status (stuck mid-process)
  • Some were in Failed status (target system not reachable or rejecting calls)
  • As of 15th March 2026, there were 1,874 stuck entries for the BW System destination alone

These stuck LUWs explained everything. Each failed or stuck tRFC entry retained its payload in ARFCSDATA indefinitely, and as BW extractions ran repeatedly over time, the number of stranded entries multiplied into the hundreds of millions.

The Fix: Scheduling RSARFCER

The resolution was to schedule the standard SAP report RSARFCER in the ECC system as a background job.

RSARFCER is SAP's built-in cleanup program for tRFC/qRFC data. It identifies and removes LUW entries that have been successfully processed or that have exceeded the configured retention period. Running this regularly is a recommended best practice but is often overlooked in busy SAP landscapes.

Tip: Schedule RSARFCER as a periodic batch job (daily or weekly depending on your RFC volume) to prevent ARFCSDATA from growing out of control.

The Results: 1.7 TB → 310 GB

The impact of the cleanup was dramatic:

MetricBefore CleanupAfter Cleanup
Total DB Size~1.7 TB310 GB
ARFCSDATA Entries84,76,26,71814,40,51,765
BW System Entries82,79,01,049Significantly reduced

The table size shrank by over 80%, freeing up more than 1.4 TB of storage — all from addressing a single table's accumulated stale data.

 

Key Takeaways

1. Monitor ARFCSDATA size proactively. Add ARFCSDATA (and ARFCSSTATE) to your regular table size monitoring. A sudden or sustained growth trend is an early warning sign of stuck tRFCs.

2. Check SM58 regularly. Stuck entries in SM58 are not just a functional problem — they are a direct driver of database bloat. Investigate and resolve them as part of your regular Basis housekeeping.

3. Schedule RSARFCER as a recurring job. This is the primary housekeeping tool for tRFC/qRFC data. It should be running on a schedule in every SAP system that uses RFC-based integrations — especially source systems feeding BW or other downstream platforms.

4. Pay attention to BW extraction RFC destinations. High-frequency BW data extractions are a common source of tRFC accumulation, particularly if the BW system experiences downtime or connectivity issues during extraction windows.

5. Investigate TID-level anomalies. If a single TID is generating millions of ARFCSDATA entries, something is fundamentally wrong with that LUW — either the extraction program is looping, or there is a data design issue creating an unusually high number of RFC calls within a single transaction.

Final Thought

ARFCSDATA bloat is one of the more underappreciated causes of unexpected database growth in SAP systems. It doesn't cause obvious application errors — it just quietly consumes space until it becomes a serious infrastructure problem. With the right monitoring and a simple scheduled job, it's entirely preventable.

If your ARFCSDATA table is growing unchecked, start with SM58 and RSARFCER — chances are, that's where the story begins and ends.

entry.title: An SAP Enablement Story: How Elementar and Cideon Reduced Friction in Product Development
entry.content:
Welcome to this blog post! In today's edition, I want to share a practical SAP enablement story from the German-speaking SAP community – featuring Elementar Analysensysteme GmbH and their implementation partner Cideon. This story originally aired as a special German-language episode of the Inside SAP HANA Cloud podcast.

No time to read? Listen to the full episode on https://url.sap/941u5n Spotify or https://url.sap/bq5x0x Apple Podcasts.

In this blog, you will find out how a mid-sized, owner-managed manufacturer:

  • Replaced a fragmented ERP/PDM landscape with a unified, SAP-native approach
  • Used SAP Engineering Control Center (ECTR) to eliminate synchronization overhead between product development and logistics
  • Chose a single implementation partner to reduce coordination friction and avoid the classic "blame game"
  • Built the data quality and process discipline needed to scale adoption step by step
  • Laid the groundwork for mechatronics integration and AI-enabled automation

This story is relevant for any engineering or manufacturing organization that is grappling with the handoff between product development and SAP S/4HANA – and wondering how to make that transition stick.

Meet the Participants

  • Dr. Sebastian Schmitt – Head of Product Development, Elementar Analysensysteme GmbH
  • Peter Vandrey – Head of SAP Solution Management, Cideon (SAP and Autodesk partner, specializing in PLM integrations)

Company Snapshots 

Elementar Analysensysteme GmbH is an owner-managed company headquartered in Langenselbold near Frankfurt, with around 320 employees worldwide and a second production and engineering site in Manchester. The company develops and manufactures analytical instruments for elemental analysis, stable isotope analysis, and optical emission spectrometry – built for long service lives of ten to twenty years. Their customers range from contract laboratories and pharmaceutical companies to wastewater treatment plants, food analytics operations, and research institutions. Elementar does not operate as a project business. They develop series products – which means data consistency, lifecycle traceability, and smooth handoffs between engineering and production are not optional: they are central to the business model.

Cideon has been an SAP development partner since 1996 and an Autodesk partner in parallel – covering software sales, consulting, and training. In the SAP space, Cideon's focus is on PLM integrations: specifically, the interfaces between engineering environments and SAP S/4 HANA, spanning on-premise, private cloud, and public cloud scenarios.

The Starting Point: A New ERP, No PDM, and Many Moving Parts

In 2021 and 2022, Elementar rolled out SAP S/4HANA (private cloud) – a major transition for the entire company. They were coming from a small ERP system with no clearly defined PDM system in place. In the legacy environment, data exchange between product development and the rest of the business relied on a web of manually maintained interfaces and synchronizations.

The go-live decision was deliberate: rather than waiting until every process was perfect, the team went live with SAP S/4HANA first – and then immediately launched a follow-on project to define how product development data would flow into SAP going forward. As Dr. Sebastian Schmitt puts it: "We needed to create awareness in my area around data: data quality, data management – how you handle data overall. Those are just a few of the challenges, and they shouldn't be underestimated." This step-by-step approach – stabilize first, then improve – turned out to be one of the key success factors of the entire engagement.

Success Factor #1: Listen First, Understand the Process, Then Decide on Standard

When Cideon came on board, the first priority was not to configure software. It was to understand how things actually worked. As Sebastian describes it, Cideon's team "sat down at one table and talked through: how do workflows and processes work today, and how should they improve? Then they listened a lot at first, which I personally appreciated – because we could describe the current situation and where we had seen gaps and problems with the old systems."

Only after that listening phase did the team begin mapping what could stay close to standard – and where adjustments were genuinely necessary. This approach avoided two common failure modes: over-customizing too early (before understanding the real process) and under-delivering (implementing a generic solution that doesn't fit the actual workflow). The principle here is broadly applicable: the best SAP implementations start with process transparency, not feature deployment.

Success Factor #2: One Partner, One Accountability

A recurring theme in Elementar's story is the conscious decision to work with a single implementation partner – one who could cover both the CAD/Autodesk side and the SAP side. In Sebastian's words: "In the past we always had many partners we had to work with, connect with each other, or who communicated with each other through us. We didn't want those friction losses anymore."

From Cideon's perspective, this structure also changed the dynamics of the project itself. Peter explains: "The kind of blaming no longer happened. So it wasn't 'the other partner from the CAD side is to blame,' or 'no, it's SAP.' None of that." Instead, both sides worked from a shared understanding of the target process – and took joint ownership of outcomes.

For organizations evaluating implementation partners, this is a practical point worth taking seriously: the coordination overhead of managing multiple vendors is not just an administrative burden. It creates real technical risk, because nobody has a complete picture of the end-to-end process.

Why SAP Engineering Control Center (ECTR) Made the Difference

The concrete tool at the center of this story is SAP Engineering Control Center. For Elementar, ECTR addressed a specific and fundamental challenge: moving data between product development and SAP without running it through external interfaces. In the legacy world, engineers worked in a PDM system and then "transferred things into an ERP system" – a phrase that covers a large amount of manual effort, synchronization logic, and potential for error. With two engineering sites (Langenselbold and Manchester), harmonizing versioning rules, release workflows, and data structures across that landscape was especially complex.

The insight that changed the picture came when the team stopped thinking of ECTR and SAP as "two separate systems talking to each other" – and started recognizing that they share one data foundation. Sebastian describes the practical result: "When you want to release a material, in the end it's one click, not a synchronization via complex interfaces." Engineers no longer notice the automation running in the background – which is exactly the point. The system supports the process without adding visible overhead.

This shift – from interface-heavy synchronization to a shared data foundation – is the core of what ECTR enables. And it has downstream effects: fewer integration points to maintain, less manual reconciliation, and more time for the actual work of product development.

What's Next: Mechatronics, More Automation, and AI

Both Elementar and Cideon are clear about the direction of travel.: he next frontier is mechatronics: bringing mechanical and electrical engineering data together in a unified model. As Peter puts it, "Engineering Control Center is well suited for that, because it allows me to connect multiple systems and link data from both systems." For a company like Elementar – which builds highly complex analytical instruments where "one doesn't work without the other" – this is a natural next step.

Sebastian adds a broader point about automation: the goal is to reduce the time engineers spend manually assembling and searching for information. "Now we can say: the system supports us, and we can focus much more on the tasks we actually need to do as a company."

AI is also on the horizon – specifically, the ability to run analyses against data that already exists in the system. Rather than treating AI as a separate capability to be added later, both speakers frame it as a natural extension of the data discipline and system foundation already being built.

One important principle that Cideon emphasizes for organizations just starting this journey: don't try to do too much at once. "First you should make sure that, on the customer side, users can cope with the system – learn to work with it – rather than implementing so much from the start that nobody knows what the system is doing in the background. First really learn to work with the system, then bring in improvements step by step."

This is enablement as a practice, not a one-time event.

Enablement Takeaways

  1. Start with understanding, not features. Listen to the current process, make gaps visible, and only then decide what can stay standard and what must be adapted.
  2. Reduce friction through clear accountability. A single partner who covers the full scope eliminates the coordination overhead and blame dynamics that derail complex implementations.
  3. Data quality is a prerequisite, not an afterthought. Product data needs ownership, shared definitions, and active governance – or the processes built on top of it won't scale.
  4. Use integration to eliminate synchronization. The value of ECTR becomes tangible when approvals and releases happen directly in the system – not through interfaces.
  5. Enablement is iterative. Build user confidence first. Then expand capability step by step, especially in complex engineering and logistics scenarios.

I hope you liked this summary of our special German-language episode of the Inside SAP HANA Cloud podcast.

Stay curious and keep tuning in! Follow me on LinkedIn and stay up-to-date with our latest enablement assets: https://www.linkedin.com/in/annette-garcia-lorenzo.

entry.title: Why Knowledge Transition Looks Complete but Still Fails After S/4HANA Go-Live
entry.content:
Why Knowledge Transition Looked Complete but Still Failed After Our S/4HANA Go-Live

Introduction

Over the years, I’ve been involved in many SAP AMS transitions, covering everything from S/4HANA and ECC to Basis operations and production support handovers. One thing I’ve noticed time and again is that knowledge transition (KT) often ends up feeling like just another box to tick.

Sessions get done, documents get uploaded, trackers turn green, and everyone feels ready to move into support. But in one of my S/4HANA projects, I learned the hard way that ticking all the KT boxes on paper doesn’t always mean the AMS team is truly prepared for the realities of production.

This experience changed how I now approach KT, especially for complex Finance environments with lots of interfaces.

The Project Situation

This project involved:

  • SAP S/4HANA implementation
  • FI integration with external accounting systems
  • Multiple inbound interfaces using IDocs
  • Batch job-heavy month-end processing
  • AMS takeover after hypercare

I was coordinating between the project team, Basis team, and AMS support teams to ensure support readiness. During KT, everything seemed very organised. The project team gave detailed walkthroughs covering:

  • FI posting flows
  • IDoc architecture
  • Background job schedules
  • Monitoring activities
  • Common error handling steps
  • Escalation contacts

The Basis team also explained standard monitoring transactions and daily health checks.

By the end of KT:

  • All KT trackers were marked complete
  • Sign-offs were obtained
  • Shadow support was completed
  • Readiness status was green

At that point, everyone felt confident.

What Happened After Go-Live

 The system was stable for the first few weeks after go-live. But the real challenge hit during the first month-end closing cycle.

I still remember one late-night support call when the finance team reported delays in accounting postings coming from external systems. At first, nothing seemed critical:

  • No dumps in ST22
  • No major system alerts
  • Background jobs were running
  • Interfaces were technically active

The AMS team’s first thought was, “The system looks fine. Let’s check with the project team.” But as we dug deeper, we noticed unusual queue behaviour:

  • During one month-end cycle, we noticed nearly 8,000 inbound FI IDocs sitting in status 64 for more than 20 minutes.


     

    Example of inbound FI IDocs waiting in status 64 during peak month-end processing.


  • SM58 showed growing tRFC entries

Example of queue buildup observed during high-volume month-end processing.

  • SMQ1 queues gradually building up
  • Some FI-related background jobs that normally finished within minutes were taking significantly longer during peak load.

The tricky part was there was no outright system failure. The issue crept in slowly under peak load. While the AMS team knew the monitoring tools and technical setup, they weren’t fully confident whether this was just normal temporary load or an early warning of a bottleneck. That uncertainty delayed troubleshooting and escalation decisions.

What We Realised Was Missing in KT

After things stabilised, we reviewed the KT approach and realised something important:

The KT sessions had focused heavily on system design and support steps, but not enough on how the system actually behaves in real production.

We covered:

  • Configuration walkthroughs
  • Interface structures
  • Monitoring steps
  • Job schedules
  • Known issue handling

But we hadn’t talked enough about:

  • System behaviour during peak month-end load
  • Queue buildup patterns seen in performance testing
  • Critical timing overlaps between interfaces and batch jobs
  • Temporary manual monitoring done during hypercare
  • Known “sensitive windows” during closing cycles

I remember a project team member saying, “We saw similar queue buildup during testing, but it cleared automatically, so we didn’t think it was a big risk.”

That stuck with me. If we had openly shared that during KT, the AMS team could have reacted much faster in production.

What We Changed in Later Projects

After that, we changed how we handle KT in future S/4HANA projects. Instead of just transferring system knowledge, we started focusing on operational realities.

During KT, we began asking questions like:

  • Where did the system struggle during testing?
  • Which interfaces are most sensitive during peak load?
  • Which batch jobs often overlap?
  • What temporary monitoring was needed during hypercare?
  • What warning signs should AMS teams watch for?

From the Basis side, these discussions became especially valuable. We started documenting:

  • Expected queue growth patterns
  • Critical SM58 monitoring scenarios
  • High-risk job execution windows
  • Typical month-end workload spikes
  • Situations where manual intervention was needed before

We also improved shadow support. Instead of immediately passing issues back to the project team, we encouraged AMS teams to try initial troubleshooting themselves, with project teams available in the background. It slowed things down at first but built much stronger support confidence after go-live.

Key Learning

The biggest lesson I learned is that knowledge transfer isn’t just about explaining the system. It’s about sharing real operational experience.

Documents can cover configurations, transactions, and monitoring steps, but true production readiness comes from understanding how the system behaves under pressure, during busy periods, and when things don’t go as planned.

In most projects, that knowledge stays with the project team unless someone makes a conscious effort to share it during KT.

Conclusion

In many SAP projects, KT is treated as a milestone to tick off before AMS takeover. But from what I’ve seen, successful KT depends less on how many documents are shared and more on honest conversations about real operational behaviour.

Sometimes the most valuable KT discussions aren’t about system architecture at all. They’re about questions like:

  • Where did we struggle during testing?
  • What nearly broke during hypercare?
  • Which processes make the team nervous at month-end?

Those conversations prepare AMS teams far better than any checklist can.

In my experience, KT truly succeeds only when teams share not just system knowledge, but also the operational judgement built through the project journey.

entry.title: SAP Sapphire 2026: Unlocking Next-Level Innovation to AI with SAP Business AI Platform
entry.content:
*Updated on May 15 with SAP Business AI Platform

Hello SAP Community!

Mark your calendars – SAP Sapphire 2026 is here, and it's set to redefine what's possible. This year, the conversation isn’t just about digital transformation. The focus is on mastering your platform journey to drive continuous innovation, achieve measurable business outcomes, and on how organizations should leverage artificial intelligence. It’s going to be a fantastic opportunity to learn, share together, and of course, I’m especially looking forward to celebrating with everyone at the afterparty. I just found out there’s hazelnut chocolate fruit waffles at Magic Kingdom, so I’m extra excited!

From AI-powered development and intelligent automation to seamless integration and strategic platform management, SAP’s Business AI platform (SAP BAIP) stands as the essential foundation. Its unique differentiation lies in deeply embedding AI across your operations while preserving the invaluable data and business application context, thereby turning challenges into tangible opportunities. Sapphire 2026 will be the pivotal event to launch SAP’s strategic vision, highlighting how AI innovations are integral to the enterprises of tomorrow.

 

The Mandate: Connecting Everything, Across the Entire Landscape

In an era when disconnected landscapes continue to hinder progress, SAP’s customers have consistently shown that the platform's true power lies in end-to-end connectivity. Whether it's your CHRO leveraging SAP SuccessFactors employee data with procurement data for newly onboarding employees, or your CFO optimizing financials with SAP S/4HANA together with external third-party benchmark data, or your CPO driving procurement excellence by keeping warehouse data up to date from supply chain partners, the challenge remains: how do you connect these vital functions, including between the AI agents, to move beyond implementation to true business transformation?

This year, you’ll see how SAP Business AI platform serves as the critical foundation for SAP’s business applications, making this connectivity a reality. It's designed to streamline operations, foster innovation, and enable real-time analysis of insights across your entire business landscape.

 

Spotlight on Transformation: Breakthrough Use Cases for a Connected Future

This Sapphire, we're particularly excited to unveil new use cases that exemplify the SAP Business AI platform’s power to address today's most pressing business challenges.

 

1. Integrate HR data and processes with government and third‑party systems

  • The Challenge: Navigating complex and evolving HR regulations, increasing demands for efficiency in HR service delivery, and managing diverse workforces effectively.
  • How the SAP BAIP helps: By integrating HR data in SAP SuccessFactors (or elsewhere) and processes with government and third-party systems through SAP Integration Suite, organizations can ensure compliance with global and local regulations, automate processes, and streamline interactions with third-parties. This connected process frees the HR department from administrative burdens, creating streamlined employee experiences and compliant data.
  • Recommended sessions at Sapphire:
    • Proving the business value of standardizing on SAP Integration Suite | BTP2350: Hear how teams quantify business value after standardizing on SAP Integration Suite: before-and-after metrics, faster time to integrate, fewer incidents, lower operating effort, and readiness for a mixed SAP and third-party software landscape. 
      🗓️ADD NOW
    • PPG Industries: Building an AI-ready, clean core integration foundation | BTP2638: See how APIs, events, and cloud integration reduce complexity, improve visibility, and establish an AI-ready foundation for future innovation across SAP and third-party systems. 
      🗓️ADD NOW
    • Building a foundation for AI with an integrated core HR system | HCM1742: Learn how a connected approach to core HR, payroll, workforce management, and HR service delivery can help you streamline your people and business processes while enabling AI readiness. 
      🗓️ADD NOW

2. Drive profitable growth with secure, personalized digital experiences

  • The Challenge: Organizations struggle with complex processes and fragmented access to customer data, facing increasing competition and rising customer expectations. Data silos often lead to slow and reactive decision-making, impacting customer satisfaction and growth.
  • How SAP BAIP helps: SAP Customer Identity and Access Management (SAP CIAM) protects sensitive customer data and meets global compliance requirements. By consolidating and integrating customer data across various systems with SAP’s platform, it enables the creation of secure, personalized digital experiences that overcome data silos. This supports dynamic and tailored customer interactions, enhancing customer acquisition and retention.
  • Recommended sessions at Sapphire:
    • Standardize, secure, and scale with core platform capabilities | BTP1315: Learn how application lifecycle management, security, and interoperability unlock SAP’s Platform to drive agility and growth. 
      🗓️ADD NOW
    • Invisible security: SAP Customer Identity and Access Management | BTP1316: Discover how SAP Customer Identity and Access Management accelerates your business by harnessing AI-assisted risk analysis to stop threats in real time, protecting customers behind the scenes without disrupting experiences. 
      🗓️ADD NOW
    • Secure by design: How SAP is redefining agent execution | BTP2641: Learn how SAP is developing the secure execution layer, combining runtime containment with the governance of AI-enabled data fabric solutions from SAP to define production-ready agentic AI.
      🗓️🗓ADD NOW

 

3. Leverage AI Capabilities in ABAP Cloud Development

  • The Challenge: Developers struggle to balance rapid delivery with maintaining code standards, while traditional coding, debugging, and testing consume a significant portion of their time. Complex legacy code also slows productivity.
  • How SAP BAIP helps: Leveraging AI capabilities directly within ABAP Cloud Development, using tools like SAP Joule for Developers, can dramatically increase developer productivity. AI assists with code generation, ensures code quality, and helps your development teams understand complex systems, freeing them to focus on innovation.
  • Recommended sessions at Sapphire:
    • Agentic ABAP with SAP Joule for Developers: Build faster, migrate smarter | JOU1428: Find out how AI agents with ABAP simplify migration, speed up coding, and increase productivity, even in older versions of SAP S/4HANA. 
      🗓️ADD NOW
    • ABAP Cloud: Entering the new era of ABAP development | BTP2107: The ABAP Cloud development model, agentic development experience in VS Code, the SAP Joule for Developers add-on, and agentic custom code migration join forces for the next era of growth in ABAP development tools. 
      🗓️ADD NOW
    • Introduction to ABAP Cloud supported by generative AI | BTP1429: Learn what ABAP Cloud is, get insights into the latest technology advancements, and see how generative AI boosts your adoption of ABAP Cloud. 
      🗓️ADD NOW

 

4. Modernize On-Premise Integration to Cloud with PI/PO to SAP Integration Suite Migration

  • The Challenge: Organizations still using SAP Process Integration/Process Orchestration (SAP PI/PO) are facing the impending need to modernize the integration methods. With a complex integration landscape comprising multiple integration solutions, not only does it add complexity, but it also creates greater technical debt.
  • How SAP BAIP helps: Migrating from SAP PI/PO to SAP Integration Suite provides a modern, cloud-native platform that reduces Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) across the integration landscape. This transition can cut the time to set up new interfaces and unlock significant annual cost savings. It's a strategic move that not only streamlines operations but also provides the scalable, agile integration backbone essential for future AI-driven cloud transformation.
  • Recommended sessions at Sapphire:
    • Modernize your strategy: Migrating from PI/PO to SAP Integration Suite | BTP1360: Learn how the suite enables smooth, scalable, and intelligent integrations across cloud and hybrid landscapes. 
      🗓️ADD NOW
    • Under Armour: Enabling real-time commerce with SAP Integration Suite | BTP2138: Under Armour is consolidating MuleSoft, Boomi, and SAP Process Integration and SAP Process Orchestration into SAP Integration Suite, advanced event mesh on SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP).
      🗓️ADD NOW
    • Modernize from SAP Process Integration and SAP Process Orchestration | BTP2100: Learn about migration approaches, coexistence options, and governance—plus the benefits customers see after moving, such as faster change cycles and better reliability and monitoring.
      🗓️ADD NOW

 

Mastering Your SAP Business AI Platform Journey: Measure, Optimize, and Guide


To truly maximize your investment in SAP BAIP, it’s crucial to understand where your organization currently stands and how to strategically advance. Sapphire 2026 will highlight the tools and methodologies designed to guide this journey, available on SAP Discovery Center: 

  • SAP Discovery Center: As your central hub for knowledge and hands-on learning, the Discovery Center continues to evolve. Discover, evaluate, and adopt SAP’s Platform and AI to create tailored business solutions and accelerate your platform adoption.

  • Maturity Assessment: Introduced at last year’s SAP TechEd, this comprehensive technical evaluation transforms uncertainty into actionable insights. In just 10-15 minutes, you can get a precise understanding of your organization's platform maturity across five levels, from "Getting Started" to "Center of Excellence." It provides a clear roadmap, justifies further investments, and uncovers hidden efficiencies and innovation opportunities.

We have since expanded the portfolio with new Line of Business AI assessments for domain-specific insights, and an AI Maturity Calculator is in development with IDC that not only assesses your platform AI readiness but also benchmarks you against your industry peers.

  • Recommended sessions at Sapphire:
    • Your platform journey: Maturity assessment and Adoption Best Practices | BTP1549: Explore the new maturity assessment that benchmarks your current state and uncovers optimization opportunities, paired with the proven platform adoption framework for SAP BTP that guides implementation.  
      🗓️ADD NOW
    • BTP101: Comprehensive Overview of SAP’s Platform — Foundations, Development, Integration, and Governance | ASUG105: This intensive 6-hour workshop provides a complete foundational overview of SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) and its core capabilities. Participants will explore the BTP ecosystem, from architecture and development frameworks to enterprise integration and data management solutions.
      🗓️ADD NOW

Your Strategic Next Steps at Sapphire 2026: Attend Must-Attend Sessions:

This Sapphire, don't just attend – Seize this opportunity to proactively empower your organization by understanding the strategic future of AI. In addition to use-case specific recommendations, I’ve identified essential sessions to deepen your strategic understanding.

Secure your spot: add these pivotal sessions to your agenda to fully grasp SAP’s AI and platform vision for enterprise innovation.

  • Scaling AI in cloud ERP with SAP's platform | BTP1343: Learn how the AI platform helps your AI in cloud ERP move from experiments to real, repeatable results. 
    🗓️ADD NOW
  • Using autonomous finance to jump-start an intelligent enterprise | BTP1345: Hear how SAP Integration Suite, SAP Build solutions, and the SAP Business Data Cloud solution offer the framework to orchestrate cross-enterprise processes, helping them steer the corporate growth plan. 
    🗓️ADD NOW
  • SAP's AI platform: What's new, what's next, and why it matters | BTP1448: Get a tour of the latest innovations, hands‑on tools for developers and business users, and an early preview of upcoming capabilities shaping the future of AI on the platform. 
    🗓️ADD NOW
  • Design the future of enterprise AI agents with Joule Studio | BTP1449: Discover how Joule Studio enables teams to shape the next era of intelligent enterprises. With an intuitive no‑code experience, create secure, context‑aware AI agents that plan, reason, and act across SAP solutions and beyond.
    🗓️ADD NOW
  • From idea to impact: Orchestrating across the AI agent lifecycle | BTP1450: Get a tour of the latest innovations, hands‑on tools for developers and business users, and an early preview of upcoming capabilities shaping the future of AI on the platform. 
    🗓️ADD NOW
  • The enterprise path to agentic AI | BTP1454: Discover how Joule Studio creates AI solutions from extensions to agentic apps. SAP’s strategy delivers an enterprise-grade intent-to-execution platform that is purpose-built for SAP customers. 
    🗓️ADD NOW
  • Unlock platform value with AI at Cardinal Health and Johnson Controls | BTP1959: Hear how Cardinal Health Inc. and Johnson Controls International plc unlock platform value with AI-enabled application development, integration, automation, and AI services.
    🗓️ADD NOW

See you there!

Sapphire 2026 is your opportunity to discover how SAP Business AI Platform can propel your organization forward to AI adoption, helping you become a truly connected enterprise with governance, ready for whatever the future holds. If you’re at Sapphire, please come see me at booth 152 – BTP1958 and let’s chat!

entry.title: Reducing Structural Friction in SAP FICO to Unlock Real Business Value
entry.content:
FICO is at the center of all SAP financial transactions and most of what a business has to work with to determine their financial status as well as how they are operating effectively and efficiently. So, in a perfect world, it would be very easy to see what is happening within a business from a financial point of view. But, in reality, this does not always happen that way.

Based on what we have seen over time, rarely has SAP FICO not performed as intended. It works, and it works very well and that's why we keep on using it. However, the initial design of the SAP FICO module can be changed by the evolution of the overall system and by numerous decisions made by many different people throughout the years. In this way, the initial structure becomes increasingly complicated, which results in finance teams having greater challenges with the way they are currently able to conduct their daily operational activities.

SAP FICO defines the organization of financial data in a variety of ways. It's not just about keeping transactions but also about having consistency with how those transactions will be organized, controlled and interpreted throughout your organization. When this organization is inconsistent, the ramifications are far reaching and will impact processes, reporting and decision making outside of the system.

In most cases, consistency doesn't happen over night but is gradually built upon over time from various sources. New customizations are made to meet a specific need in the organization, integrations are added as more systems come online and processes are modified to fit the business need. Each of these changes are fine in isolation; however, over time they start to create a level of structural friction that makes things a little more difficult than necessary.

Frictions are easily noticeable in the sense that they start to create sitations nobody really wants to deal with: financial closes take longer to complete, reports need to be manually adjusted prior to being able to rely on them, teams spend more time reconciling the same numbers rather than analyzing them, and when someone needs a concise snapshot of their financials across multiple areas, what should be simple now becomes considerably more complicated.

One project we did with a large energy firm illustrates the same point. We spent time working with their sap systems to organize and combine finance-related data from the many disparate sources of SAP data. Over years of implementation, a large amount of data built up in an uncoordinated manner, and therefore created many inconsistencies across the company.

At first, it appeared that the issue might be due to the data not being available, but that wasn't entirely accurate. Data was in fact present, but in many different formats, extracted by various means, and not at the same level of detail. Many of the departments did not use the same data, nor did they use it consistently.

Related to that, basic analytic activities became very challenging. Every report had to be reformatted and validated, and the time taken to make decisions moved from immediate to much longer due to the effects of the system. Nevertheless, the organization of data within the system created a high level of friction throughout all levels of the company.

In one of our projects, an example of what we found was that operational data coming from the field was fed directly into SAP (the operating system for supply chain management) and automatically caused maintenance and work order processes to be triggered without manual involvement. After establishing the backbone of that integrated process, the end-to-end flow of information could occur without the need for any further validation thereby allowing the process to be completed more quickly and reliably.

It quickly became apparent that this type of friction is very common and that the reason for this kind of friction is generally structural. At its root, we find a human issue since the evolution of systems tends to be a result of different teams making decisions that don’t follow a single, well-thought-out plan.

To ease this friction, you don’t necessarily need to redesign everything from scratchr. In most instances, it would be more beneficial to simplify what already exists. For example, if you align key data structures, eliminate non-essential complexity, and ensure that integrations are based on a consistent logic, they will have a substantially greater positive effect than providing additional tools or layers on top.

SAP FICO is a strong starting point for running finance at present, and the challenge is whether or not it should continue to add on top of it as well as keep the support models for SAP FICO consistent over time.

When the support models are clear, it is easier to trust the data from SAP FICO, and the processes will function smoothly, allowing the teams to make decisions rather than fixing inconsistencies in the system. When they are not, even the simplest tasks will require additional steps, which makes it harder for a user to see value in the system. Our experience shows that growing complexity causes decreased business value, not increased business value.

entry.title: Modernizing Healthcare ERP Systems (SAP S/4HANA) to Strengthen Operations and Support Better Care
entry.content:
Why I Took This Project

When I was asked to lead this SAP S/4HANA rollout for a U.S.-based medical device company, the decision wasn't difficult. Medical devices directly affect patients. When the systems behind those devices work well — when inventory is accurate, when compliance is airtight, when the right product reaches the right place at the right time — patients benefit. That connection between back-end ERP operations and real patient outcomes was what made this project meaningful to me from day one.

What I didn't fully appreciate when I said yes was just how complex the journey from 2020 to 2022 would be. Multiple U.S. business units. A fresh S/4HANA template built from scratch. Nine external system integrations. A 50-person team. SOX compliance requirements. And the kind of data challenges that don't reveal themselves until you're deep in System Integration Testing wondering what just broke and why.

I'm glad I took it. But there are things I would do differently — and that's really what this post is about.

What We Were Actually Trying to Do

The organization had multiple U.S. business units operating independently, each with its own processes, systems, and way of doing things. The goal was straightforward in concept: bring all of them onto a single SAP S/4HANA platform, unit by unit, using a fresh template built specifically for this program.
